perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will commence upon the Effective Date and will continue for an initial term of one (1) year, unless earlier terminated as provided herein (the “Initial Term”).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will commence upon the Effective Date and will continue for an initial term of ten (10) years, unless earlier terminated as provided herein (the “Initial Term”).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the initial term to ten years could create issues if state laws regarding contract enforceability limit the duration of certain types of agreements, or if it conflicts with reasonable business practices, making the agreement potentially unconscionable or subject to stricter scrutiny.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds - Contracts that cannot be performed within one year
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds requires contracts that cannot be performed within one year to be in writing to be enforceable. While this agreement is likely to be in writing, a ten-year term might be viewed as unusually long and subject to greater scrutiny regarding enforceability, depending on the specific subject matter of the contract and applicable state law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. (1) Except as otherwise provided in this section a contract for the sale of goods for the price of $500 or more is not enforceable by way of action or defense unless there is some writing sufficient to indicate that a contract for sale has been made between the parties and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought or by his authorized agent or broker. A writing is not insufficient because it omits or incorrectly states a term agreed upon but the contract is not enforceable under this paragraph beyond the quantity of goods shown in such writing. (2) Between merchants if within a reasonable time a writing in confirmation of the contract and sufficient against the sender is received and the party receiving it has reason to know its contents, it satisfies the requirements of subsection (1) against such party unless written notice of objection to its contents is given within 10 days after it is received. (3) A contract which does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (1) but which is valid in other respects is enforceable (a) if the goods are to be specially manufactured for the buyer and are not suitable for sale to others in the ordinary course of the seller's business and the seller, before notice of repudiation is received and under circumstances which reasonably indicate that the goods are for the buyer, has made either a substantial beginning of their manufacture or commitments for their procurement; or (b) if the party against whom enforcement is sought admits in his pleading, testimony or otherwise in court that a contract for sale was made, but the contract is not enforceable under this provision beyond the quantity of goods admitted; or (c) with respect to goods for which payment has been made and accepted or which have been received and
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the initial term of the agreement to ten years, which could violate the Statute of Frauds. The Law Explanation points out that contracts not performable within one year must be in writing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from the Uniform Commercial Code § 2-201, supports this, stating that contracts for the sale of goods for $500 or more are unenforceable unless there is sufficient writing. While the agreement is likely in writing, the ten-year term introduces a significant enforceability risk, especially since no Scraped Law Snippet provides exceptions or clarifications for such extended durations. The initial term extension significantly increases the likelihood of the agreement falling under the Statute of Frauds, thereby creating a substantial legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon five (5) days prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to five days may be problematic. Depending on the specific nature of the contract, applicable state laws may require a longer notice period to allow the other party sufficient time to make alternative arrangements or mitigate potential losses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires that parties act honestly and fairly in the performance and enforcement of a contract. A five-day notice period might be considered unreasonably short and a breach of this covenant, especially if the agreement involves significant ongoing obligations or investments.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a 30-day notice period for termination, while the changed text reduces this to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation states that a shorter notice period could be problematic under state laws if it doesn't allow sufficient time to make alternative arrangements or mitigate potential losses. The Law Explanation suggests this could breach the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing if unreasonably short. While the provided snippets do not directly state any law, the Perturbation Explanation suggests that a 5-day notice period could be considered insufficient under applicable state laws, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: All notices under this Agreement must be in writing and delivered by personal delivery, reputable overnight courier, or certified mail, return receipt requested, to the addresses set forth above.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices under this Agreement must be delivered via telepathy to the designated contact.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying 'telepathy' as a method for delivering notices is not legally valid or enforceable. Contract law requires methods of notice that provide reasonable assurance of receipt and documentation, which telepathy does not provide. This change introduces a term that is clearly not legally sound.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Requirement of Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 68 dictates that notice must be reasonably communicated. Telepathy is not a recognized or reliable means of communication, making it an unreasonable and unenforceable method for delivering notices under the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: UCC 1-207 | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you UCC 1-207 [In 2004 UCC 1-207 was changed to UCC 1-308 .] Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies legally recognized methods for delivering notices (personal delivery, overnight courier, certified mail), ensuring a verifiable record of receipt. The changed text replaces these methods with 'telepathy,' which is not a legally recognized or reliable means of communication. The Law Explanation states that Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 68 dictates that notice must be reasonably communicated, and telepathy fails this standard. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is about UCC 1-207 (later UCC 1-308) and seems irrelevant on its face, the Law Explanation combined with the Perturbation Explanation make clear that a valid legal contradiction exists, making 'YES' the correct answer.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of [State], without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Planet Mars, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing the law of 'Planet Mars' as the governing law makes the agreement unenforceable. Contracts must be governed by the laws of a recognized legal jurisdiction. Referring to a non-existent jurisdiction renders the governing law clause invalid.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws dictates that the law chosen must bear a reasonable relationship to the contract and not violate the public policy of the forum. The laws of Mars clearly fail to meet either criterion.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Commercial Code - Uniform Law Commission Skip to main content (Press Enter). Sign in Skip auxiliary navigation (Press Enter). Follow ULC Schedule of Events Better Laws. Stronger States. Skip main navigation (Press Enter). Uniform Commercial Code Summary The Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) is a comprehensive set of laws governing all commercial transactions in the United States. It is not a federal law, but a uniformly adopted state law. Uniformity of law is essential in this area for the interstate transaction of business. Because the UCC has been universally adopted, businesses can enter into contracts with confidence that the terms will be enforced in the same way by the courts of every American jurisdiction. The resulting certainty of business relationships allows businesses to grow and the American economy to thrive. For this reason, the UCC has been called “the backbone of American commerce.” History The Uniform Law Commission was formed in 1892 in part to create uniform commercial laws. The Uniform Negotiable Instruments Law was approved in 1896, and soon enacted in every state. More commercial laws soon followed: the Uniform Sales Act and Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act in 1906; the Uniform Bills of Lading Act and Uniform Stock Transfer Act in 1909; and the Uniform Conditional Sales Act in 1918. The ULC officially took on the task of drafting a comprehensive code to provide guidelines for all commercial transactions in 1940. In 1942, the ULC and the American Law Institute joined in a partnership that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently with
perturbation.justification: The change from 'State of [State]' to 'Planet Mars' directly contradicts the implicit requirement that contracts must be governed by the laws of a recognized legal jurisdiction, as described in the Perturbation Explanation. Although the provided snippets do not explicitly prohibit selecting laws from non-existent jurisdictions, the UCC snippet states that it governs 'all commercial transactions in the United States' and that uniformity of law is essential 'for the interstate transaction of business.' This implies a system of laws from established jurisdictions, not fictional ones.

perturbation.original_text: The failure of either party to enforce any provision of this Agreement will not constitute a waiver of future enforcement of that provision or any other provision.
perturbation.changed_text: The failure of either party to enforce any provision of this Agreement will automatically and irrevocably waive that provision for all future instances.
perturbation.explanation: Changing this clause to automatically waive any provision not enforced contradicts standard contract law principles regarding waiver. Generally, waiver requires clear and intentional relinquishment of a known right. An automatic waiver due to non-enforcement could have unintended and detrimental consequences, particularly if the non-enforcement was due to oversight or temporary circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: Common law defines waiver as the intentional relinquishment or abandonment of a known right or privilege. The modified text creates an *automatic* and *irrevocable* waiver, contradicting the requirement for intentional relinquishment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. [Last reviewed in July of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal procedure business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure individual rights legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates an automatic and irrevocable waiver, which directly contradicts the common law definition of waiver as "intentional relinquishment or abandonment of a known right or privilege." The scraped law snippet from Wex defines a waiver as "the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege." By making the waiver automatic, the modified text removes the requirement for intentional action, thereby contradicting established legal principles regarding waiver.
